grounding_note: Expansion batch: more employer-identity and injury-nature disclosures.

scenarios.opening_query: I got hurt loading freight and the claims process has been a runaround from day one.
scenarios.plausible_initial_labels: Workers' Comp > State || Workers' Comp > Federal
scenarios.hidden_fact: I'm a civilian materials handler at the Army depot — federal employee, so it's the federal comp system.
scenarios.fact_as_answer: I'm a civilian handler at the Army depot — federal employee, federal comp system.
scenarios.relevant_question_topic: whether the employer is the federal government
scenarios.counterfactual_hidden_fact: I load for a regional grocery distributor — ordinary state comp.
scenarios.counterfactual_fact_as_answer: Regional grocery distributor — ordinary state comp.
scenarios.vetting_note: Civilian-military-employer variant of the federal split.

scenarios.opening_query: Since the robbery at my store I can't sleep, can't focus, and now I can't work either.
scenarios.plausible_initial_labels: Workers' Comp > Stress Related || General Litigation > Crime Victims
scenarios.hidden_fact: They caught the robber and he's being sentenced — the victim advocate says I can pursue restitution and a civil claim against him, which is what I want alongside anything work-related.
scenarios.fact_as_answer: They caught him — he's being sentenced, and I want the victim restitution and civil claim against him.
scenarios.relevant_question_topic: whether a civil claim against a caught perpetrator is the goal
scenarios.counterfactual_hidden_fact: He was never caught — my fight is the comp carrier denying my PTSD claim as 'not a physical injury.'
scenarios.counterfactual_fact_as_answer: He was never caught — my fight is the comp denial of my PTSD claim.
scenarios.vetting_note: Perpetrator-caught fact opens the crime-victim route; otherwise the stress-claim fight.

scenarios.opening_query: My husband works the docks and got crushed between a container and the bull rail last month.
scenarios.plausible_initial_labels: Workers' Comp > LHWA || General Litigation > Maritime
scenarios.hidden_fact: He's actually a crewman assigned to the ship, not shore labor — he sails with it, which everyone says makes him a seaman under maritime law.
scenarios.fact_as_answer: He's crew assigned to the ship — he sails with it, which makes him a seaman under maritime law.
scenarios.relevant_question_topic: whether the worker is a vessel crewman or shore-based longshoreman
scenarios.counterfactual_hidden_fact: He's shore-based — a longshoreman on the pier, never part of any crew, which is the LHWA system.
scenarios.counterfactual_fact_as_answer: He's shore-based longshore labor — never crew. That's the LHWA system.
scenarios.vetting_note: Seaman-status is the classic maritime jurisdictional fact.

scenarios.opening_query: My employer never had insurance for my injury and now acts like that's my problem.
scenarios.plausible_initial_labels: Workers' Comp > State || Workers' Comp > Employers
scenarios.hidden_fact: I should explain — I'M the employer in trouble here too: I'm the injured working owner, we never carried comp on ourselves, and the state is citing MY company for the coverage gap while my own claim floats.
scenarios.fact_as_answer: It's complicated — I'm the injured working owner. We never carried comp on ourselves, and the state is citing my company for the gap while my own claim floats.
scenarios.relevant_question_topic: whether the user is an injured worker or a cited uninsured employer (or both)
scenarios.counterfactual_hidden_fact: I'm just an employee — the state's injured-worker fund is handling my claim while they chase my uninsured boss.
scenarios.counterfactual_fact_as_answer: I'm just an employee — the state fund has my claim while they chase my uninsured boss.
scenarios.vetting_note: Owner-operator dual posture is stated openly by the user; the disclosure resolves which side needs counsel first.
